title: Filtering Alongside Vector Search
concept: Combining a nearest-neighbor query with structured filters like date range, category, or user permissions.

exercises.prompt: Welcome to Hybrid Metadata Filtering! As we begin, how do you think vector search and metadata filters work together?

options.text: Vector search finds conceptual similarity, while metadata filters apply strict rules.
Vector search and metadata filters are the exact same thing.
Metadata filtering completely replaces the need for vector databases.

exercises.explain: This is the first lesson of the unit on Hybrid Metadata Filtering, introducing how structured filters (like category or date) work alongside vector search.

cards.title: The Limits of Pure Vector Search
cards.body: Vector search is great at finding matching concepts, but it doesn't understand structured rules on its own. If you ask for 'cheap hotels' using only vectors, you might get luxury suites because they are still 'hotels'. We need a way to combine similarity with hard filters!

cards.title: What is Metadata Filtering?
cards.body: Metadata filtering is the process of restricting a vector search using structured criteria—like tags, dates, or permissions—so you only search through a relevant subset of your database.

cards.title: The Library Usher
cards.body: Imagine you are looking for a mystery novel in a giant library. Vector search finds books with a similar 'vibe' (nearest neighbors), while metadata filtering is like immediately ignoring any book not written in English or published before 2020.

cards.title: Real-World Filtering
cards.body: If a user asks 'Show me sports news from yesterday', we convert 'sports news' into a vector to search for meaning, and apply a structured metadata filter where 'category' equals 'sports' and 'date' is 'yesterday'.

cards.title: Vectors Don't Do Math Well
cards.body: Many believe vector search naturally understands dates and prices if you write them in text. In reality, vectors are bad at precise math; structured filters are required to guarantee exact matches for rules like 'price < 50'.

cards.title: Pure Search vs. Hybrid Filtering
cards.body: See how combining semantic vector similarity with structured metadata helps target the exact documents you need.

left.text: Finds articles with a similar vibe to 'tech news', but might return old or restricted files.

right.text: Finds 'tech news' vectors, but strictly limits the search to 'Year: 2023' and 'Access: Public'.

exercises.prompt: If you want to find recipes similar to 'healthy lunch ideas' that are also strictly 'under 500 calories', how should you design the query?

options.text: Using only vector search since vectors automatically filter numbers perfectly.
Combine a vector search for 'healthy lunch ideas' with a metadata filter where calories are less than 500.
Convert the number 500 into a word and search for it using only keyword matching.
Use metadata filtering to find the word 'healthy' and ignore the vector database.

exercises.explain: Vector search handles the abstract meaning ('healthy lunch ideas'), while structured metadata filters handle the hard rules ('under 500 calories').

exercises.prompt: True or False: Combining vector search with metadata filtering allows you to restrict search results based on user access permissions.
exercises.explain: Metadata filtering allows you to apply strict criteria (like user permissions or dates) directly to your vector search, ensuring you only retrieve matching documents that the user is allowed to see.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the description of combining search criteria:
exercises.sentence: To prevent an AI from reading private files, we apply metadata filters to restrict the search to public documents.
exercises.explain: Metadata filters are structured constraints (like category = 'cooking') applied to a vector search to restrict the pool of documents to a specific subset.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes how an AI assistant executes a hybrid metadata search?

options.text: I will search the database for all documents containing the words 'puppy', 'training', 'category', and '2021'.
I will retrieve all documents from the database, sort them by date, and then show you the ones about puppies.
I will execute a vector search for 'puppy training' filtered by category = 'pets' and publish_year > 2021.

exercises.explain: Option C represents an AI-generated response where the system correctly performs a semantic search (finding 'dog training') while respecting the exact structured metadata constraints (matching 'category' and 'published after 2021').

exercises.prompt: You are building an HR assistant. A user asks: 'What is our vacation policy?' You want to find documents similar to this query, but only search documents that are currently 'active' and marked 'public'. What is the correct way to handle this?

options.text: Run a vector similarity search for 'vacation policy' on all documents in the database without any metadata constraints.
Search for the nearest vectors to 'vacation policy' while filtering where status equals 'active' and visibility equals 'public'.
Retrieve all documents created in 2023 and sort them alphabetically by their title.
Search only for documents that contain the exact word 'vacation' in their title and ignore vector embeddings.

exercises.explain: To ensure we only search within active, public documents, we must apply the metadata filters (status = active AND visibility = public) alongside our semantic vector search. Options B, C, and D either miss the metadata constraints entirely or do not combine them with vector similarity.
